danger_signals.signal: Carnival setting with escaped dangerous creature
danger_signals.visual_form: Bruno the bigfoot (frozen specimen, later animated threat)
danger_signals.meaning: Primary mystery/danger driver—creature is both objective and potential hazard
Opposition to player goals; captures bigfoot as competitive threat

danger_signals.visual_form: Dead-end scenarios (mitigated by design—player cannot truly fail)
danger_signals.meaning: Logical threat to progress, but not to character survival

safety_signals.meaning: Establishes non-lethal, comedic world; violence is absurdist (Max used as tool)

safety_signals.visual_form: Point-and-click interface with multiple interaction modes
safety_signals.meaning: Safe exploration—cursor mode changes signal available actions, reducing unintended harm
Safe transport between locations; anchors player in controllable space

faction_indicators.symbol_or_sigil: Detective/vigilante identity; dog and rabbit anthropomorphic pair
faction_indicators.environmental_markers: New York City office as home base; DeSoto serves as mobile headquarters

faction_indicators.enemy_visual_variants: Conroy Bumpus as competing captor (country western persona)
faction_indicators.environmental_markers: Carnival grounds; distributed tourist attractions across American landscape
Absent from visual space—communicates only via voice/text

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Carnival setting presented as primary environment; frozen bigfoot display now empty
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Sam and Max respond to Commissioner directive; investigate carnival grounds

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Map UI expands to show additional tourist trap locations across USA
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Player gains ability to fast-travel via DeSoto; new dialogue options unlock regarding Bruno sightings

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Additional creature encounters at distributed locations; pattern of liberation emerges
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: NPCs react with alarm; quest complexity increases with parallel objectives

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Country western character appears with Bruno in custody
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Direct conflict emerges; player must solve puzzles to recover target from rival

environmental_storytelling.example: World's Largest Ball of Twine, vortex with subterranean magnets, Mount Rushmore bungee—Americana clichés communicate world as exaggerated Americana landscape
1960 DeSoto Adventurer visual presence establishes era (1960s-inspired setting) and serves as safe home base from which player branches into dangerous comedic scenarios
Sam (dog) and Max (rabbit) anthropomorphic pairing immediately communicates 'cartoon logic world' vs. realistic threat modeling
Carnival grounds contain exposition NPCs, initial quest hook (Bruno's escape), and visual mystery (empty display); establishes detective procedural grammar

environmental_storytelling.technique: Dialogue tree icons as state communication
environmental_storytelling.example: Conversation tree at screen base with icon-based topic selection allows players to read available plot threads visually without text parsing

entity_behavioral_tells.behavior: Walks, talks, examines objects; cursor changes when hovering interactable entities
Walks around areas independently; can be activated via inventory icon for problem-solving (typically violent solutions)
Freed from carnival captivity; actively traveling with Trixie; later recaptured by Conroy
Freed Bruno as act of love; accompanies him in flight from carnival
Intercepts Bruno; holds him for competitive purposes; presents obstacle to player resolution
Report missing attractions; provide initial quest exposition; remain stationary at carnival location

notes: Sam & Max Hit the Road uses cartoon visual language to communicate safety despite adventure-game danger framing. The 2D art style, anthropomorphic characters, and LucasArts design philosophy (player cannot die/reach dead-end) all work together to establish a 'consequence-free' world where danger signals are narrative hooks rather than survival threats. Faction communication relies on location association (carnival vs. tourist traps vs. New York office) rather than color-coding or sigil systems. World-state progression is communicated through UI expansion (map reveals new locations) and NPC dialogue shifts, creating a procedural detective narrative. Environmental storytelling emphasizes Americana parody as thematic vehicle—the game's world is a collection of tourist-trap clichés that both ground the setting and signal absurdist tone.
